text: After placing the trochars, the surgeon examines the stomach, which appears patulous and dilated. The first and beginning of the second portion of the duodenum are also observed to be significantly dilated due to chronic obstruction from superior mesenteric artery syndrome.

The surgeon lifts the transverse colon to reveal the ligament of Treitz. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels are visible, and the third and fourth portions of the duodenum appear decompressed.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel and sharp dissection to expose the C-loop of the duodenum, preparing for the Coker maneuver. The transverse colon is lifted to reveal the underlying dilated portion of the duodenum.

The surgeon demonstrates the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels causing vascular compression of the duodenum, leading to outlet obstruction. The jejunum, approximately twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz, is selected and brought up as an anticholic limb.
The surgeon places a stay suture in the selected portion of the jejunum and attaches it to the C-loop of the duodenum. A posterior row of running 3-0 sutures is hand-sewn to secure the connection.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy on the small bowel for a planned side-to-side anastomosis, followed by an enterotomy of equal length on the duodenal side.
The surgeon measures the anastomosis to be five centimeters in total and begins suturing the inner layer using micro sutures.
The surgeon completes the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis using running 3-0 vacuo-lumbar sutures, finalizing the two-layered anastomosis between the jejunum and duodenum.

The surgeon completes the two-layered hand-sewn functional side-to-side anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum. An endoscopic view reveals a wide-open anastomosis, confirming the patency of the connection.
